Termite season varies by region but typically peaks during swarm season (spring in southern markets, late spring/early summer in northern markets). Termite work generates the highest per-customer revenue in the industry ($1,500-$3,500 per treatment + warranty contracts).

Rodent control runs counter-cyclical — peaks in fall (October-December) as rodents seek shelter from cold weather, with secondary spring peak. Northern markets see steady rodent demand year-round.

Winter is slowest for general pest but still active for rodent and German cockroach work. Many operations use winter for recurring contract renewals, route optimization analysis, and training.

Hurricane season in coastal markets (June-November) drives post-storm pest spikes as displaced populations migrate into structures.

vertical_faqs.q: Can general FSM tools (Jobber, Housecall Pro) work for pest control?
vertical_faqs.a: They work for very small operations (1-2 techs, <100 customers) but struggle past that. Pest control is fundamentally a recurring-route business, not a job-by-job dispatch business. Specialty pest platforms (GorillaDesk, ServSuite, PestPac, FieldRoutes) handle route optimization, recurring billing automation, chemical logging compliance, and tier-specific service plans natively in ways general FSM tools can't replicate.

vertical_faqs.q: What's the difference between PestPac and FieldRoutes?
vertical_faqs.a: Both are mid-to-enterprise pest platforms acquired by WorkWave. PestPac is the longer-established option with deeper enterprise features and broader install base. FieldRoutes (formerly Aptive's internal tool, now sold separately) has more modern UX and better integration with door-to-door sales workflows. Both handle the core pest workflow (routes, recurring billing, chemical logging) well; pick based on UX preference and integration needs.

vertical_faqs.q: How does chemical logging compliance actually work in software?
vertical_faqs.a: Pest-specialty platforms log each application: EPA registration number, product name, target pest, area treated, amount used, concentration, applicator (with license number), date, weather conditions, and outcome. Audit-ready reports can be generated for state inspections. General FSM tools can't do this — you'd be manually maintaining spreadsheets, which fails an audit fast.

vertical_faqs.q: What about door-to-door sales workflows?
vertical_faqs.a: FieldRoutes has the strongest D2D-focused workflow, designed around Aptive's sales model: rep knocks door, signs contract on mobile device, customer record creates automatically, recurring billing starts, first service auto-scheduled. PestPac has D2D support but less optimized. ServSuite and GorillaDesk are weaker on D2D. If door-to-door is 30%+ of your customer acquisition, this matters.

vertical_faqs.q: How important is route optimization for pest control?
vertical_faqs.a: Critical — directly drives revenue per tech per day. A 15% improvement in route efficiency translates to 15% more billable stops per day. Pest platforms with purpose-built route optimization (FieldRoutes, PestPac, GorillaDesk) beat anything you'd get from general FSM tools. The math at scale (10+ techs running daily routes) makes the platform investment trivial relative to the productivity gains.

verticals.name: Pool Service
verticals.industry_context:
The US pool service industry generates roughly $5 billion annually across 8,000+ businesses, dominated by route-based recurring service operations. Pool service is fundamentally a subscription business — 80%+ of revenue comes from recurring weekly or bi-weekly service plans rather than one-time work. This makes it operationally similar to pest control: route optimization, recurring billing, and chemical handling compliance drive economics.

Geographic distribution matters more than in most trades. Pool service is concentrated in warm-weather markets (California, Florida, Texas, Arizona, Nevada) and seasonal markets in the rest of the country. Year-round markets have steadier operations; seasonal markets see dramatic spring-summer revenue concentration with winter shutdown or reduced service.

Skimmer dominates this niche — purpose-built pool service software with route optimization, chemical tracking, photo documentation of pool conditions, and recurring billing. ServiceTitan, Jobber, and Housecall Pro work for small pool operations but struggle past 100 customers because the recurring-route model isn't their core design.

Service plans are typically $80-$200 per month for weekly service in residential markets. Higher-end markets ($300-$500/month) include premium equipment monitoring, automated maintenance, and faster response time. Software that handles tier-specific service routing (basic plan customers get standard frequency; premium customers get priority and additional services) drives meaningful revenue per customer.

Equipment sales and repair (pumps, filters, salt cells, heaters) drive higher per-customer revenue. Software that integrates parts inventory with service routing helps techs respond to equipment issues efficiently.
verticals.buying_personas:
Pool service buyer profiles:

Solo or 2-tech pool service operators buy software to manage 50-200 recurring customers, automate billing, track chemical applications, and reduce missed visits. Decision in 2-4 weeks. Skimmer (pool service specialty) typically wins for serious operators because the chemical tracking and pool-specific workflow saves real time vs general FSM. Solo operators on tighter budgets sometimes use Jobber Core but eventually upgrade as customer count grows.

Established 5-15 tech pool service operations are buying to scale routes across 300-1,500+ customers with consistent chemical compliance documentation and reliable recurring billing. Skimmer dominates this segment. Some operations use PoolFront or Pool Brain as alternatives. The differentiator: route optimization sophistication, chemical compliance depth, and photo documentation of pool conditions.

Large multi-location pool service operations or franchise systems (Anthony Sylvan, Premier Pools, etc.) use enterprise versions of Skimmer or custom solutions. The PE-backed roll-up trend in pool service (Premier Pools acquired by Sun Capital, others) is driving demand for multi-location enterprise platforms.
verticals.feature_priorities:
Five features matter disproportionately in pool service:

Route optimization for weekly recurring routes — directly drives revenue per tech per day. A 15% improvement in route efficiency translates to 15% more billable stops per day. Skimmer has purpose-built route optimization that beats anything general FSM tools can do for the pool-route model.

Chemical handling and pool condition tracking — pool service techs apply chlorine, algaecides, pH adjusters, and other chemicals at each visit. Software that logs each application (product, amount, conditions found, conditions after treatment) drives quality service and provides liability protection. Skimmer has this baked in; general FSM tools require workarounds.

Photo documentation of pool conditions — recurring customers benefit from photo documentation of pool conditions at each visit (water clarity, equipment status, debris). Software with crew mobile workflows for organized photo capture drives customer retention by demonstrating consistent service quality. Specialty pool platforms have this; general FSM tools have photo support but less workflow integration.

Recurring billing for service plans — running 200-1,500+ recurring customers on monthly billing requires reliable automation, failed-payment retry workflows, and plan upgrade/downgrade handling. Skimmer and other pool platforms handle this natively.

Equipment service integration — pool equipment failures (pumps, filters, salt cells, heaters) require service calls separate from routine maintenance. Software that integrates equipment service routing with the recurring maintenance routing helps techs respond to issues during their normal route rather than as one-off visits.
verticals.regulatory_notes:
Pool chemical handling — pool service operators are subject to OSHA HAZCOM requirements for chemical storage, training, and labeling. SDS sheets for all products in use must be available. Some states require additional pool chemical applicator licensing.

Pool barrier compliance — some jurisdictions require pool service operators to flag barrier/safety compliance issues during routine service. Software with checklist support helps.

Workers comp classification — pool service has moderate WC rates due to chemical exposure and slip-and-fall risks.

State pesticide licensing — some pool chemicals are classified as pesticides in certain states; commercial operators may need pesticide applicator licensing.

Local water-use regulations — pool drainage and refill activities may have local restrictions; software that surfaces requirements helps.
verticals.seasonality:
Northern markets see dramatic seasonality:

Spring opening (March-May) is the biggest revenue spike — pool opening service, equipment startup, repair work for issues discovered during opening. Customer acquisition surges as homeowners seek service operators for the season.

Summer peak (June-August) is recurring weekly/bi-weekly service plus chemical balance, equipment repair, leak detection. Highest operational complexity of the year.

Fall closing (September-November) brings the second revenue spike — winterization, equipment shutdown, cover installation. Recurring billing typically pauses or reduces for closed pools.

Winter (December-February) is dramatically slower in northern markets — many operations shut down completely or shift to indoor pool maintenance only.

Southern markets see year-round demand but still see modest seasonal patterns (summer recurring service plus repair work).

vertical_faqs.q: Should pool service operators use Skimmer or general FSM software?
vertical_faqs.a: Skimmer wins for serious pool service operations (100+ customers) because the chemical tracking, route optimization, and pool-specific photo documentation save real time vs general FSM. Solo operators under 50 customers can use Jobber Core or Housecall Pro Basic effectively. Crossover point is typically 100 customers — past that, the pool-specific workflow features pay off.

vertical_faqs.q: How important is chemical tracking for pool service?
vertical_faqs.a: Critical for liability protection and customer quality. Logging each chemical application (product, amount, pool conditions before/after) protects against chemical-imbalance liability claims and demonstrates service quality to customers. Specialty pool platforms (Skimmer, Pool Brain) have this baked in; general FSM tools require manual logging that fails compliance audits.

vertical_faqs.q: Can pool service software integrate with QuickBooks?
vertical_faqs.a: Yes — Skimmer integrates with QuickBooks Online for accounting sync. The high transaction volume (thousands of recurring billings per month for mid-size operations) makes deep QB integration essential. Manual sync isn't feasible at scale.

vertical_faqs.q: How do pool service operations handle equipment service vs routine maintenance?
vertical_faqs.a: Best-practice operations integrate equipment service routing with their recurring maintenance routing — equipment issues identified during routine service get scheduled into the same tech's upcoming route rather than as one-off visits. Skimmer handles this natively; general FSM tools treat equipment service as separate work orders, creating less efficient routing.

vertical_faqs.q: Is pool service software different in seasonal vs year-round markets?
vertical_faqs.a: Yes. Year-round markets (Florida, Arizona) have steadier operations with consistent recurring billing. Seasonal markets need software that handles pool opening/closing workflows (seasonal contract suspension, equipment winterization, spring restart). Skimmer handles both well; general FSM tools struggle with the seasonal suspension and restart workflows.

verticals.name: Locksmith
verticals.industry_context:
The US locksmith industry generates roughly $3 billion annually across 12,000+ businesses, dominated by on-demand emergency service work. Lockout calls (home, car, business) drive 60-70% of residential locksmith revenue, with the remainder from new lock installation, rekey work, key duplication, and commercial security work. Average per-job revenue is $50-$300 for residential service, $200-$1,500 for commercial security work.

Workiz is the dominant FSM platform for locksmiths — purpose-built for on-demand service businesses with native call handling, AI dispatcher, and Google Local Services integration. The industry has specific software needs that general FSM tools don't fit well.

The market is highly fragmented with intense competition from scammer operations (call-and-quote $39 locksmiths who arrive and charge $500+) that have damaged industry reputation. Legitimate locksmiths increasingly compete on transparent pricing, fast response time, and Google Local Services Ads (with the green-check verification badge). Software with strong call handling, transparent pricing display, and GLS integration drives competitive advantage.

Automotive locksmith work (programming key fobs, car key duplication, lockout assistance) is increasingly specialized as modern vehicles use complex transponder systems. Operators specializing in automotive work need software that tracks vehicle make/model/year and key compatibility.
verticals.buying_personas:
Locksmith buyer profiles:

Solo locksmiths buy software to manage on-demand call intake, dispatch, and basic billing. Decision in 1-2 weeks. Workiz fits well; some use Jobber Core or Housecall Pro Basic but lose the on-demand optimization. Many solo operators also depend heavily on Google Local Services Ads for lead generation, so software with strong GLS integration is critical.

Established 3-10 tech locksmith operations (often covering metro markets with multiple techs across geography) buy to scale dispatch routing and reduce response time. Workiz dominates this segment because of the AI dispatcher and on-demand call handling. The differentiator: how fast you can dispatch the closest available tech to each call.

Larger locksmith operations and commercial security operators (50+ techs, multiple branches, focus on commercial access control + security installations) use enterprise versions of Workiz, ServiceTitan with locksmith configuration, or specialty commercial security platforms. Commercial security work (access control systems, video surveillance, intrusion alarms) often uses specialized security industry platforms rather than locksmith-focused FSM.
verticals.feature_priorities:
Five features matter disproportionately in locksmith services:

On-demand call handling and AI dispatcher — locksmith work is response-time-critical (customer locked out of car at 11pm needs help in 30 minutes, not 3 hours). Native phone systems with call routing, AI dispatchers that route to closest available tech, and AI phone agents for after-hours overflow drive competitive advantage. Workiz has the strongest on-demand features.

Native Google Local Services Ads integration — GLS dominates top-of-page Google results for locksmith queries with the green-check verification badge. Native integration (Workiz) attributes booked jobs to specific ads automatically. Operations without strong GLS presence lose to scammer operations and competitors with stronger GLS attribution.

Transparent pricing display — legitimate locksmiths compete against scammer call-and-quote $39 operations by displaying transparent pricing on websites and in-app quotes. Software that supports clear pricing displays and itemized quotes helps customer trust.

Vehicle/make/model tracking for automotive locksmiths — modern car key programming requires accurate vehicle identification. Software that tracks vehicle data and key compatibility helps techs respond efficiently.

Mobile-first dispatch workflow — locksmith techs operate from vehicles constantly. Software with strong mobile UX for accepting calls, navigating to job sites, completing work, and collecting payment without office back-and-forth drives productivity. Workiz and specialty locksmith tools have this; general FSM tools are adequate.
verticals.regulatory_notes:
State locksmith licensing — required in some states (Texas, California, Tennessee, others) with background check requirements. Multi-state operations need to track licensing.

Background check requirements — many jurisdictions require locksmiths to pass background checks given access to homes and vehicles.

Bonding and insurance — most states require locksmiths to be bonded and insured given the trust required.

Commercial access control regulations — fire code requirements for commercial door hardware (panic hardware, accessible egress), increasingly mandated by inspectors.

Vehicle key programming compliance — modern automotive locksmith work involves access to manufacturer-restricted programming tools; misuse can have legal implications.

Privacy regulations — locksmiths access homes and vehicles; documentation of service work and consent matters for liability protection.

OSHA compliance — moderate risk for locksmith work; documentation matters.
verticals.seasonality:
Locksmith demand is largely year-round with modest seasonal variation:

Summer brings increased vacation-related lockouts (homeowners locked out returning from vacation, second-home locksmith work in resort markets) plus increased car lockouts (parking lots, beach access).

Winter brings increased frozen-lock work in cold markets, broken-key incidents from cold weather, and weather-related lockouts.

Spring and fall are steady demand with modest variation.

Late-night and weekend demand drives premium pricing — software that tracks pricing variations by time-of-day and day-of-week helps optimize revenue.

Hurricane/storm seasons in coastal markets see brief spikes in lockout work as displaced residents lose keys.

vertical_faqs.q: Why is Workiz dominant for locksmith software?
vertical_faqs.a: Workiz is built for on-demand service businesses — exactly the locksmith model. Native phone system with call routing, AI dispatcher that routes calls to closest available tech, Google Local Services Ads integration, and mobile-first dispatch workflow all fit locksmith work better than general FSM tools designed around scheduled-recurring service.

vertical_faqs.q: How important is Google Local Services Ads for locksmiths?
vertical_faqs.a: Critical. Locksmith customers searching at 11pm for emergency service mostly see Google Local Services Ads with the green-check verification badge at the top of results. Operations without strong GLS presence compete against scammer call-and-quote operations and lose. Native GLS integration with software that attributes booked jobs back to specific ads helps optimize spend.

vertical_faqs.q: What's the typical response time for locksmith service?
vertical_faqs.a: Industry standard is 30-60 minutes for residential lockouts in metro markets, 1-2 hours for non-emergency work. Software that minimizes time between call intake and tech dispatch (AI dispatcher matching closest tech, mobile workflow eliminating office back-and-forth) drives competitive advantage in the response-time game.

vertical_faqs.q: Should locksmith operations focus on residential, commercial, or automotive?
vertical_faqs.a: Most operations do all three but increasingly specialize. Residential lockouts have lower revenue per job but highest volume. Commercial access control work has higher revenue but longer sales cycles. Automotive work has highest skill barrier (modern key programming) but premium pricing. Software that handles tracking across all three (vehicle data, commercial customer relationships, residential routing) helps multi-vertical operations.

vertical_faqs.q: How do legitimate locksmiths compete against scammer operations?
vertical_faqs.a: Transparent pricing (display rates upfront, itemized quotes), strong online reputation (Google reviews, BBB accreditation), Google Local Services verification with the green-check badge, and clear branding (vehicle wraps, uniforms, identification). Software supports this with transparent in-app quoting, automated review collection, and GLS attribution.

verticals.name: Garage Door
verticals.industry_context:
The US garage door industry generates roughly $3 billion annually across 10,000+ businesses, split between residential service work (broken springs, opener repair, panel replacement — typical ticket $200-$800) and installation work (full new garage doors, $800-$3,500 typical residential installation). The industry has consolidated significantly as manufacturers (Clopay, Wayne Dalton, CHI, Amarr) acquire installation networks and as PE-backed operators (A1 Garage Door Service, Precision Garage Door, others) roll up regional independents.

Workiz and ServiceTitan compete for garage door software, with each serving different segments. Workiz dominates smaller on-demand-focused operations (lots of residential service work, fewer installations) because of its on-demand workflow optimization. ServiceTitan serves larger operations doing significant installation work because of pricebook depth, marketing attribution, and consumer financing integration for the bigger-ticket replacement jobs.

Spring repair is the highest-volume service work — typical residential garage door spring fails every 7-10 years, generating 30-50% of typical operator residential service revenue. Software with parts inventory for common spring sizes (and the ability to order specialty springs as needed) helps tech response.

Insurance and warranty work generates additional revenue stream — broken garage doors from car impacts (homeowner insurance claims) and warranty work for manufacturer-defective openers (Liftmaster, Genie warranty programs).
verticals.buying_personas:
Garage door buyer profiles:

Solo or 2-3 tech garage door operators buy software to manage on-demand service dispatch, parts inventory, and basic billing. Decision in 2-3 weeks. Workiz fits well; some use Jobber Core or Housecall Pro Basic. Solo operators often run primarily service work (spring repair, opener repair) with installations as secondary revenue.

Established 5-20 tech garage door operations doing mixed service + installation work are buying to scale dispatch and capture more installation revenue via consumer financing for big-ticket replacements. Decision in 2-3 months. Workiz Standard/Pro or Housecall Pro Essentials/MAX typically wins. The differentiator: handling the mix of routine service + emergency overflow + big-ticket installation quoting.

Large multi-region garage door operations (50+ techs, PE-backed roll-ups) use ServiceTitan (residential trades focus) with garage door configuration. The PE-backed roll-up trend in garage door (A1 Garage Door Service, Precision Garage Door, others) drives demand for enterprise platforms with multi-location reporting and standardization features.
verticals.feature_priorities:
Five features matter disproportionately in garage door:

On-demand dispatch for service work — spring failures and opener issues are urgent for customers (can't park car, can't access home). Software with native call handling and fast dispatch drives customer satisfaction. Workiz and ServiceTitan have native dispatch optimization.

Consumer financing for installations — new garage door installations ($800-$3,500 typical) convert significantly better with in-app financing offers. Closes 25-40% more replacement jobs at the moment of decision. ServiceTitan's FinanceIt and Housecall Pro's Wisetack both work; native financing beats third-party bolt-ons.

Parts inventory for common service parts — garage door springs (standard 2 inch diameter, varies by torsion vs extension type) and opener parts (Liftmaster, Genie components) need to be in-truck for most service calls. Software that tracks vehicle inventory and surfaces needed parts during dispatch saves return-visit work.

Manufacturer warranty integration — Liftmaster and Genie warranty programs require specific documentation and claim submission. Software that handles warranty work alongside non-warranty service helps maximize warranty revenue.

Photo documentation of installations — new garage door installations benefit from before/after photos (proves quality of work, creates marketing assets). Software with structured photo capture during installation drives customer satisfaction and provides portfolio for sales.
verticals.regulatory_notes:
State contractor licensing — varies; some states (California, Florida) require garage door contractor licensing for installation work above modest thresholds.

UL/CSA certification compliance — garage door openers must meet UL 325 safety standards (photo-eye sensors, force reversal). Software that surfaces compliance requirements helps avoid installations of non-compliant systems.

Local permits — garage door replacement may require permits in some jurisdictions; software with permit tracking helps avoid lapses.

Workers compensation classification — moderate to high WC rates due to spring tension risks during repair (improperly handled springs can cause serious injury). Documentation of safety training matters.

Insurance claim documentation — garage door damage from car impacts requires homeowner insurance documentation; software with photo capture and claim documentation helps customer process.
verticals.seasonality:
Garage door demand sees moderate seasonal patterns:

Spring brings increased service demand as homeowners discover spring-related issues after winter use, plus increased installation work as homeowners do exterior upgrades.

Summer is steady demand with increased installation work (favorable weather for installations) and ongoing service work.

Fall is the second installation peak as homeowners prep for winter, plus increased service demand from cold-weather-related opener issues.

Winter brings increased emergency service (frozen rollers, opener failures in cold weather) but reduced installation work in northern markets.

Storm seasons in coastal markets see brief spikes from wind-damaged doors. Spring-loaded door failures often spike in seasonal transitions.

vertical_faqs.q: What's the best garage door software?
vertical_faqs.a: Workiz for smaller on-demand-focused operations (lots of residential service work). ServiceTitan for larger operations doing significant installation work (pricebook depth, financing integration, marketing attribution). The crossover point is typically 8-10 techs or $1M annual revenue. Below that, Workiz; above that, ServiceTitan becomes increasingly justified.

vertical_faqs.q: How important is consumer financing for garage door installations?
vertical_faqs.a: Very important — installations ($800-$3,500) convert 25-40% better with in-app financing offers. Many homeowners who would defer or shop the project commit on the spot when monthly payment options ($50-$150/mo) are presented. ServiceTitan's FinanceIt and Housecall Pro's Wisetack both work; native financing beats third-party bolt-ons.

vertical_faqs.q: How do garage door operations handle parts inventory?
vertical_faqs.a: Most operations stock common parts in trucks: standard springs (2 inch diameter torsion springs in common lengths), opener components (Liftmaster and Genie remotes, photo-eye sensors), and miscellaneous parts (cables, brackets, hinges). Specialty springs and non-standard parts are ordered as needed. Software with vehicle inventory tracking helps techs see parts availability before dispatching.

vertical_faqs.q: Should garage door operators focus on service or installation?
vertical_faqs.a: Most successful operations do both. Service work provides baseline volume and routine revenue ($200-800 per job, frequent). Installation work provides higher per-job revenue ($800-3,500) but less frequent. The mix typically runs 70/30 service-to-installation by job count, but 40/60 by revenue. Software that handles both workflows efficiently maximizes operations.

vertical_faqs.q: Why is the PE roll-up trend in garage door significant?
vertical_faqs.a: PE-backed operators (A1 Garage Door Service, Precision Garage Door, others) are acquiring regional garage door operations aggressively. This drives standardization on enterprise platforms (ServiceTitan with garage door configuration), multi-location reporting needs, and consistent customer experience across geographies. Independent operators competing against PE-backed competitors benefit from professional software (Workiz Pro+) that matches enterprise capabilities at lower cost.

verticals.name: Appliance Repair
verticals.industry_context:
The US appliance repair industry generates approximately $5 billion annually across 30,000+ businesses, with most operations serving residential customers on demand. The business model is similar to plumbing or locksmith — customer calls when something breaks (refrigerator stops cooling, washer leaks, oven won't heat), tech responds within 24-48 hours, diagnoses issue, quotes repair, completes work or orders parts.

Manufacturer warranty work is a major revenue stream for many operations. Authorized service relationships with Whirlpool, GE Appliances, Samsung, LG, and others provide steady warranty-claim work paid by the manufacturer rather than the homeowner. ServiceBench dominates this niche — manufacturers' preferred service-routing platform. Independent operations with warranty authorization run ServiceBench alongside their general FSM platform.

Parts availability is a central operational challenge. Modern appliances often need specific OEM parts that may have 3-7 day lead times. Software that integrates parts inventory with service scheduling helps techs determine on first visit whether parts are needed, then schedule return visits efficiently. Workiz, ServiceTitan, and specialty appliance repair tools handle this; general FSM tools require workarounds.

The industry has been challenged by short replacement cycles for modern appliances. As appliance prices have come down and quality has declined for some categories, repair work often competes with replacement. Software that helps techs calculate repair vs replacement decisions for customers builds trust and longer-term customer relationships.
verticals.buying_personas:
Appliance repair buyer profiles:

Solo or 2-3 tech appliance repair operators buy software to manage scheduling, dispatch, and parts ordering. Decision in 2-3 weeks. Workiz fits well here (on-demand workflow), as does Jobber Core/Connect. Solo operators with manufacturer warranty work also use ServiceBench alongside their primary FSM.

Established 5-15 tech appliance repair operations are buying to scale dispatch across multiple techs with parts inventory coordination. Decision in 2-3 months. Workiz, Housecall Pro Essentials, or Jobber Connect typically win. The differentiator: parts inventory integration, manufacturer warranty routing, and recall/safety alert handling.

Large appliance repair operations (20+ techs, multi-region) sometimes use ServiceTitan with appliance repair configuration or specialty appliance platforms. Multi-location operations doing warranty work for multiple manufacturers run ServiceBench centrally with their primary platform handling non-warranty work.
verticals.feature_priorities:
Five features matter disproportionately in appliance repair:

Parts inventory integration — most appliance repairs require specific OEM parts with 3-7 day lead times. Software that integrates parts catalog (Marcone, V&V Appliance Parts, etc.) with service scheduling helps techs determine on first visit whether parts are needed and schedule return visits efficiently. Specialty appliance platforms and ServiceTitan handle this; general FSM tools require workarounds.

Manufacturer warranty integration (ServiceBench) — authorized warranty service is a major revenue stream. Software that integrates with ServiceBench for warranty claim routing, parts authorization, and claim submission saves real time vs juggling ServiceBench separately.

Diagnostic time tracking — appliance repair visits typically include diagnostic time (often unbilled or partially-billed) plus repair time. Software with separate tracking helps measure technician productivity and identifies trainings opportunities.

Photo documentation of model/serial — repair work requires accurate appliance identification (model, serial, capacity). Software with structured photo capture of unit nameplates speeds parts ordering and prevents wrong-part shipments.

On-demand dispatch with skill matching — different techs have different brand/category expertise (refrigeration vs laundry, gas vs electric, commercial vs residential). Software that matches techs to jobs based on expertise drives better first-visit close rates. Workiz handles this well; general FSM tools require custom workflows.
verticals.regulatory_notes:
EPA Section 608 certification — required for techs handling refrigerants (most refrigerator and freezer repair). Some platforms track cert expirations.

State HVAC/refrigeration licensing — some states require licensing for refrigeration repair work; varies by state.

Manufacturer service authorization — voluntary but required for warranty work and provides revenue stream. Documentation of authorized status matters for customer trust.

Lemon law and warranty implications — appliance repair operations may receive subpoenas or be involved in lemon-law disputes. Software with detailed service history helps respond to inquiries.

Recall notifications — manufacturers issue recalls periodically; operations with customer records benefit from notification workflows. Software with customer database for recall outreach drives goodwill.

Workers comp — moderate rates due to lifting injuries and electrical exposure.
verticals.seasonality:
Appliance repair sees modest seasonal patterns:

Summer peak brings refrigerator and freezer repair (most failures coincide with peak loads during hot weather). Air conditioning repair within home (window units, mini-splits) overlaps with HVAC work.

Winter brings heating-appliance failures (gas ovens, ranges, holiday-season cooking-related repairs) and seasonal indoor appliance work as households use appliances more intensively.

Holiday season (November-December) sees increased demand for kitchen appliances (Thanksgiving / Christmas cooking) and emergency repair work for failures that disrupt holiday preparations.

Spring brings post-winter repair work and laundry equipment service (heavy winter use patterns).

Demand is generally year-round with modest seasonal variation, less dramatic than HVAC or landscaping.

vertical_faqs.q: Is Workiz the best software for appliance repair?
vertical_faqs.a: For most independent appliance repair operations, yes — Workiz's on-demand-first design fits the appliance repair workflow well (customer calls when broken, fast dispatch, parts ordering). Specialty appliance platforms exist but have smaller install bases. Jobber and Housecall Pro work but lack on-demand-specific features (call routing, parts inventory integration).

vertical_faqs.q: How important is ServiceBench integration?
vertical_faqs.a: Critical for operations doing manufacturer warranty work. ServiceBench is the dominant warranty service routing platform — Whirlpool, GE Appliances, Samsung, LG, and others route warranty work through it. Operations with warranty authorization typically run ServiceBench alongside their primary FSM for warranty claim handling. Few platforms integrate natively; most operations juggle the two.

vertical_faqs.q: How do appliance repair operations handle parts inventory?
vertical_faqs.a: Best-practice operations carry common parts in vans and order specific parts as needed. Software with parts catalog integration (Marcone, V&V Appliance Parts, others) helps techs identify needed parts during diagnostic visits and order from the field. Without this integration, parts ordering becomes office-staff work with delays.

vertical_faqs.q: Should appliance repair shops focus on warranty work or out-of-warranty work?
vertical_faqs.a: Depends on volume and margin. Warranty work provides steady revenue (manufacturer pays) but lower margins. Out-of-warranty work has higher margins but requires customer acquisition spending. Most established operations run both — warranty work provides baseline volume; out-of-warranty work and replacement sales provide growth.

vertical_faqs.q: How does appliance repair software handle the repair vs replace decision?
vertical_faqs.a: Most platforms don't have built-in repair-vs-replace tools. Best-practice operations use industry rules of thumb (don't repair if cost exceeds 50% of replacement cost, or appliance is past expected lifespan) and document their recommendation. Software that captures the recommendation and outcome helps build customer trust over time.

verticals.name: Handyman Services
verticals.industry_context:
The US handyman services industry generates roughly $5 billion annually across 100,000+ businesses, dominated by solo operators and 2-3 person small operations. The industry has been transformed by lead-generation platforms (TaskRabbit, Thumbtack, Handy, Angi Leads) which now drive 30-50% of customer acquisition for many independent handyman operators. This dependence on third-party lead-gen creates different software needs than other trades.

The work is multi-skill by definition — handyman operators handle plumbing repairs, electrical replacements, drywall, carpentry, painting, deck repair, fixture installation, and minor remodeling under one roof. Per-job tickets are smaller ($100-$1,000 typical) than HVAC or electrical, with higher visit frequency (multiple jobs per day vs 3-5 for traditional trades). This makes scheduling efficiency and dispatch routing more important than feature depth on any single trade.

Per-hour billing is more common than flat-rate pricing in handyman work, which differentiates software needs from HVAC/plumbing where pricebook automation is central. Software with time-tracking for billable hours, easy mobile time entry, and customer-facing time-and-materials breakdowns matters more than pricebook depth.

Lead-gen platform integration is becoming a competitive differentiator. Software that captures leads from TaskRabbit/Thumbtack/Angi automatically, converts to scheduled jobs, and tracks per-platform CAC helps operators optimize their lead-gen spend across multiple channels. Most general FSM platforms support these integrations via Zapier; few have native integration.
verticals.buying_personas:
Two buyer profiles dominate handyman services:

Solo handyman operators buy software to manage scheduling, send invoices, and accept payments. Decision in 1-2 weeks. Price sensitivity very high — solo handyman is $50-100K revenue, can't justify $200+/mo. Jobber Core ($39), Housecall Pro Basic ($65), or specialty handyman tools (Pocket Handyman, Joist) typically win. Many solo operators run multiple lead-gen platforms (Thumbtack + Angi + Yelp) and want software that consolidates leads from each.

Small 3-8 person handyman operations are buying to scale dispatch across multiple techs, manage recurring property maintenance customers (some handyman operations run recurring property care for HOAs, vacation rentals, real estate property managers), and handle the multi-trade scheduling complexity. Decision in 2-4 weeks. Jobber Connect/Grow, Housecall Pro Essentials, or specialty handyman tools win.

Larger handyman operations (10+ techs) typically transition to traditional FSM platforms (Jobber Plus, Housecall Pro MAX, Service Fusion) once they outgrow handyman-specialty tools. The transition often happens around $1M revenue or when operations expand into a specific trade vertical (becoming primarily an HVAC, electrical, or plumbing shop with handyman as a secondary service).
verticals.feature_priorities:
Five features matter disproportionately in handyman services:

Multi-trade scheduling and skill matching — handyman operators handle varied work types per day. Software that matches the right tech (skills, certifications, experience level) to each job rather than just first-available drives better customer satisfaction and reduces rework. General FSM tools handle this with custom workflows; few have it baked in.

Lead-gen platform integration (TaskRabbit, Thumbtack, Angi Leads, Yelp) — automatically capturing leads from third-party platforms, converting to scheduled jobs, and tracking per-platform CAC helps operators optimize lead spending across multiple channels. Most platforms support this via Zapier; specialty handyman tools often have native integration.

Time-and-materials billing — per-hour billing is more common than flat-rate in handyman work. Software with easy mobile time entry (start/stop timer for each work activity) and customer-facing time + materials breakdowns matters more than pricebook automation. Jobber, Housecall Pro, and specialty handyman tools all support this.

Photo documentation for repair work — handyman repairs benefit from before/after photos (proves problem existed, shows quality of work). Software with structured photo capture during the job and easy customer/portfolio export drives customer retention and creates marketing assets for social media.

Mobile-first workflow — handyman techs are in the field constantly with brief office time. Software that lets techs handle the entire job (accept lead, scope work, quote, complete, collect payment) from their phone without office back-and-forth drives productivity. Jobber's mobile UX is strongest here; Housecall Pro is solid; specialty handyman tools vary.
verticals.regulatory_notes:
State contractor licensing — handyman work typically falls under minor work exemption ($500-$5K threshold depending on state) below which no license is required. Above the threshold, state contractor licensing applies. Software that flags work scopes exceeding licensing thresholds helps avoid unlicensed contracting violations.

Workers compensation classification — handyman operations have moderate WC rates depending on actual work mix (heavier on electrical/plumbing increases rates).

Trade-specific licensing within handyman scope — electrical work above minor thresholds requires electrician, plumbing above thresholds requires plumber. Many handyman operators sub-out work that exceeds their licensed scope.

Insurance requirements — most handyman operations need general liability + workers comp; some commercial customers require bonding.

I-9 employment documentation — important for handyman operations with hired employees.
verticals.seasonality:
Handyman services see modest seasonal patterns vs other trades:

Spring (March-May) brings increased demand as homeowners tackle deferred maintenance and seasonal addition work (deck repairs, fence work, exterior touch-ups). Often the strongest revenue window.

Summer brings steady demand for indoor work as homeowners avoid the heat, plus exterior maintenance.

Fall is the pre-winter maintenance window — gutter cleaning, weatherization, exterior painting before weather changes.

Winter is generally slower in northern markets except for indoor work; mild markets see steadier demand year-round. Holiday season (November-December) sees increased decoration installation and post-holiday repair work.

Vacation rental and AirBnB-related maintenance work creates year-round demand in vacation markets.

vertical_faqs.q: What's the best handyman software for a solo operator?
vertical_faqs.a: Jobber Core ($39/mo) or Joist (free + $13/mo Pro) are the most popular choices. Joist is the cheapest legitimate option (quote + invoice only, no scheduling). Jobber adds scheduling, automated reminders, and customer portal. Pick Joist if you only need quotes/invoices; Jobber if you want scheduling and growth potential. Avoid building from scratch — even solo operators benefit from automated invoicing and payment collection.

vertical_faqs.q: How do handyman operators handle lead-gen platforms (TaskRabbit, Thumbtack, Angi)?
vertical_faqs.a: Most handyman operators run multiple lead-gen platforms simultaneously. Software with lead-capture integration (Jobber via Zapier, specialty handyman tools natively) automatically converts lead-gen submissions into scheduled jobs without manual entry. Tracking per-platform CAC across channels helps optimize lead-gen spend. Many operators eventually move away from lead-gen toward direct booking (Google, website, referrals) as their reputation grows.

vertical_faqs.q: Can handyman software handle multi-trade work (plumbing + electrical + carpentry)?
vertical_faqs.a: Yes — general FSM platforms (Jobber, Housecall Pro) handle multi-trade work fine. The bigger issue is licensing — handyman scope is typically capped at $500-$5K depending on state; work above that requires the appropriate trade license. Software that flags work scopes exceeding licensing thresholds helps avoid unlicensed contracting violations.

vertical_faqs.q: Should handyman operations use flat-rate pricing or time-and-materials?
vertical_faqs.a: Most handyman operations use time-and-materials (per-hour labor + parts markup) because the work varies widely per visit. Flat-rate pricing works for repeatable jobs (fence repair, deck staining, fixture installation) where labor estimates are predictable. Software that supports both models gives flexibility. Most handyman operators use T&M for diagnostic visits and flat-rate for known scopes of work.

vertical_faqs.q: When should a handyman operation move to a specialized trade FSM?
vertical_faqs.a: When 50%+ of revenue concentrates in one trade vertical. If you're doing increasingly HVAC, plumbing, electrical, or another specialized trade, the depth of trade-specific FSM platforms (ServiceTitan, FieldEdge, Workiz) starts paying off vs general handyman tools. The transition typically happens around $300K-$500K revenue when one trade emerges as the primary business.

verticals.name: Solar
verticals.industry_context:
Residential solar is a high-ticket, sales-and-design-heavy trade unlike most field-service work. A typical residential install runs $15,000-$40,000, and the sale hinges on a fast, accurate proposal — system size, panel layout, production estimate, savings model, and financing — produced while the homeowner is still interested. That's why the software solar contractors buy looks different from HVAC or plumbing FSM: the center of gravity is design + proposal + sales-workflow software, not dispatch. The category leaders (Aurora Solar, OpenSolar) generate 3D roof models and production estimates from satellite imagery in seconds; sales platforms (Enerflo, Solo) wrap design, financing, and contract e-signature into one lead-to-PTO workflow.

The other defining feature of solar software is financing and lender integration. Because almost every residential deal is financed, the proposal tool has to plug into multiple solar lenders (Mosaic, GoodLeap, Sunlight, and others) so reps can present monthly-payment options in the living room. Design accuracy also carries real downstream cost: an inaccurate proposal that doesn't match what the engineering and permitting teams can actually build creates change orders, cancellations, and margin erosion — which is why managed-design accuracy (Solo markets 99.9%) and permit-ready electrical output (where Aurora is weaker and shops bolt on AutoCAD or PVCase) are recurring evaluation points.
verticals.feature_priorities: Solar contractors weigh software differently than other trades. Design speed and accuracy come first — AI roof modeling from satellite or LIDAR imagery that produces a usable layout and production estimate in under a minute is table-stakes for the leaders. Financing integration is second — the proposal must present multiple lenders' monthly-payment options at the point of sale, since nearly all residential deals are financed. Proposal quality and in-conversation editing matter third — reps need to adjust system size, equipment, and pricing live with the homeowner. Permit-ready electrical output (single-line diagrams, wire sizing, voltage-drop) is a frequent gap: design-first tools like Aurora often require add-ons (AutoCAD, PVCase) for stamped permit packages. Finally, lead-to-PTO workflow — connecting sales, install tracking, and interconnection paperwork — is what platforms like Enerflo layer on top of pure design tools.

vertical_faqs.q: What software do solar installers actually use?
vertical_faqs.a: Most residential solar contractors run a design-and-proposal platform as their core tool. Aurora Solar is the market leader (AI 3D modeling, contact-sales pricing); OpenSolar offers a genuinely free full-featured alternative; Solo focuses on fast, managed-design proposals priced per proposal; and Enerflo wraps design, financing, and install tracking into one lead-to-PTO operations platform. The right pick depends on whether you want free (OpenSolar), the deepest design (Aurora), outsourced design accuracy (Solo), or a full ops platform (Enerflo).

vertical_faqs.q: Is OpenSolar really free?
vertical_faqs.a: Yes — OpenSolar's design, sales, and project-management software is free to use with no seat limits or design caps. It monetizes through hardware distributors, lenders, and finance partners who pay for placement and access to its installer network, rather than charging installers a subscription. As of 2026 it began charging for some API access and third-party connectors, but the core platform remains free.

vertical_faqs.q: Why doesn't solar software handle permits and electrical design well?
vertical_faqs.a: Most solar design tools optimize for the sales proposal (layout, production, savings), not stamped engineering. Permit-ready single-line diagrams, wire sizing, and voltage-drop calculations often require add-on tools — Aurora users, for example, commonly add AutoCAD or PVCase. If permit-ready output matters to your workflow, evaluate the electrical-design depth specifically rather than assuming the proposal tool covers it.

verticals.name: Moving
verticals.industry_context:
Moving is a high-volume, lead-driven, logistics-heavy home-service business, and the software movers buy reflects that. A mover's day is CRM and quoting at the front (leads come in fast and go cold faster, and accurate quotes on weight, volume, and distance drive margin), dispatch and crew/truck scheduling in the middle, and an electronic bill of lading (eBOL), inventory, and payment collection on the job. That's why the category has its own dedicated platforms — SmartMoving, MoveitPro, Supermove, Elromco — rather than movers using generic FSM: the workflow (instant online quotes, local vs intrastate vs interstate moves, crew apps, storage, eBOL, and valuation handling) is specific enough that horizontal tools fall short.

The economics push toward all-in-one consolidation. The typical mover runs a stack of separate CRM, quoting, dispatch, and accounting tools and spends $500-$1,000/month across them; the dedicated platforms pitch replacing that stack with one system of record. Lead-response speed is the single biggest revenue lever — moving is intensely competitive on the first callback — so the CRM and automated-follow-up tooling matters as much as the operational features. QuickBooks integration is near-universal because movers still run their books there.
verticals.feature_priorities: Movers weigh software around a specific workflow. Instant, accurate quoting comes first — local (hourly) vs long-distance (weight/volume) pricing, with online self-quote tools that capture leads. Lead management and fast follow-up is second, since moving jobs are won or lost on response speed. Crew and truck scheduling/dispatch is third — assigning the right crew size and trucks to each job. On the job, an electronic bill of lading (eBOL), inventory capture, and a crew mobile app replace paper. Payments and deposits (card on file, balance on delivery) plus QuickBooks accounting integration round it out, and storage management matters for movers offering warehousing. The dedicated platforms differ mainly on depth (SMB vs enterprise), pricing model (flat vs per-crew), and how modern the crew app and AI tooling are.

vertical_faqs.q: What software do moving companies use?
vertical_faqs.a: Most established movers run a dedicated moving-company platform rather than generic FSM. SmartMoving and Supermove target growing-to-enterprise operations with deep CRM, dispatch, and crew apps; MoveitPro is a long-standing all-in-one; Elromco is a modern all-in-one for small-to-mid movers. They handle the moving-specific workflow — instant quoting, eBOL, crew scheduling, storage — that horizontal tools don't.

vertical_faqs.q: How much does moving company software cost?
vertical_faqs.a: It varies by model. MoveitPro starts around $150/mo and Elromco around $289/mo (flatter pricing), while SmartMoving starts around $399/mo and Supermove uses per-crew pricing (Essentials ~$176/crew/mo plus ~$39/truck/mo). Expect to pay more for enterprise depth and multi-location/franchise support. Most platforms aim to replace $500-$1,000/mo of separate tools.

vertical_faqs.q: Do movers need dedicated software, or will general FSM work?
vertical_faqs.a: General FSM (Jobber, Housecall Pro) can run a very small mover, but the moving-specific workflow — weight/volume long-distance quoting, eBOL, valuation, crew-size dispatch, storage — is where dedicated platforms pull ahead. Once you're past a couple of trucks and doing long-distance or interstate moves, a moving-specific platform usually pays for itself.

verticals.name: Restoration
verticals.industry_context:
Restoration — water, fire, mold, and storm-damage remediation — is one of the most software-specific trades because it's fundamentally an insurance business. Most jobs are paid by carriers through adjusters, the estimating standard is Xactimate (Verisk), and the work is documentation-heavy by necessity: carriers require photographic proof, moisture readings, and daily drying logs to approve and pay claims. Miss the documentation and you don't get paid. That, plus 24/7 emergency response and a mitigation-then-repair job lifecycle, is why restoration has its own dedicated software category rather than running on generic FSM or construction PM.

The tools split into two roles. Field documentation platforms (Encircle is the leader) sit at the front of the job — fast mobile capture of photos, sketches, moisture maps, and contents that holds up with a carrier. Full job-management platforms (Albi, DASH, Restoration Manager) run the whole operation — scheduling, crews, equipment tracking (air movers, dehumidifiers), Xactimate and QuickBooks integration, and claim/job financials. Many restorers run a documentation tool plus a job-management platform together. Buyers range from small water-mitigation shops to large multi-location franchises (ServiceMaster, SERVPRO, PuroClean), and carrier program participation often shapes which software they can use.
verticals.feature_priorities: Restoration software is weighed against the claim. Field documentation comes first — photos, moisture mapping, drying logs, and contents capture that satisfy carrier requirements and get claims paid; weak documentation is the fastest way to lose money in this trade. Xactimate / insurance-estimating integration is second, since Xactimate is the carrier standard for estimates. Job/workflow management across the mitigation-then-repair lifecycle is third, including crew scheduling and 24/7 emergency dispatch. Equipment tracking (air movers, dehumidifiers, air scrubbers) matters because equipment on a job is billable and easy to lose. QuickBooks accounting integration and claim financials round it out, and a strong mobile app is essential because the documentation happens in the field. The dedicated platforms differ on whether they lead with documentation (Encircle) or full job management (Albi, DASH, Restoration Manager), and on how modern the experience is.

vertical_faqs.q: What software do restoration companies use?
vertical_faqs.a: Most restorers run a dedicated restoration platform, often two together: a field-documentation tool (Encircle is the leader) for carrier-ready photos, moisture maps, and drying logs, plus a job-management platform (Albi, DASH, or Restoration Manager) for scheduling, equipment tracking, Xactimate/QuickBooks integration, and claim financials. The insurance-driven, documentation-heavy workflow is why generic FSM falls short.

vertical_faqs.q: How much does restoration software cost?
vertical_faqs.a: It varies widely. Albi runs $60-$100/user/month, Restoration Manager around $225/month, and DASH from about $595/month; Encircle (documentation) is priced by jobs per year (entry around $270) with unlimited users. Many shops budget for both a documentation tool and a job-management platform, so model the combined cost against the claims revenue better documentation protects.

vertical_faqs.q: Why is documentation so important in restoration software?
vertical_faqs.a: Because carriers pay the bills. Insurers require photographic proof, moisture readings, and drying logs to approve and pay a claim, and incomplete documentation leads to denied or reduced payments. That's why field-documentation tools like Encircle are central to the restoration software stack, and why Xactimate (the carrier estimating standard) integration matters across the category.

verticals.name: Painting
verticals.industry_context:
Painting is a high-volume, estimate-driven trade, and the software painters buy reflects that the sale is won on a fast, accurate, professional-looking quote. Painters price by surface area and surface type (walls vs trim vs ceilings, interior vs exterior, prep vs paint), so the estimating engine — not dispatch — is the center of gravity. That's why painting has its own dedicated tools (PaintScout, Estimate Rocket, QuoteIQ, CorkCRM) built around how painters actually price jobs, even though plenty of painters also run generalist FSM like Jobber or Housecall Pro.

The category splits into two roles. Estimating-and-proposal tools (PaintScout, Estimate Rocket) optimize the quote: square-foot/surface pricing, polished proposals, and fast turnaround in the home. CRM/all-in-one painting platforms (QuoteIQ, CorkCRM) wrap estimating with lead pipeline, scheduling, invoicing, and review collection. Because painting is an intensely local, reputation-driven business won on Google reviews and fast follow-up, the CRM and review-automation side matters as much as the estimate. Modern entrants increasingly add AI — paint calculators, before/after image generation, and satellite measurement — to speed estimating further.
verticals.feature_priorities: Painters weigh software around the quote. Surface-based estimating comes first — pricing by square footage and surface type (walls, trim, ceilings, exterior), ideally with production-rate logic, since the estimate is where painting jobs are won or lost. Professional proposals are second — clean, branded quotes that close in the home. Lead management and fast follow-up is third, because painting is local and reputation-driven, and speed-to-quote wins. Scheduling/crew coordination and invoicing/payments round out the operation, and automated review collection matters because Google reviews drive painting leads. Newer tools add AI estimating aids (paint calculators, before/after image generation, satellite measurement). The dedicated platforms differ on whether they lead with estimating (PaintScout, Estimate Rocket) or full CRM/all-in-one (QuoteIQ, CorkCRM), and on price and how modern the experience is.

vertical_faqs.q: What software do painting contractors use?
vertical_faqs.a: Many painters run a dedicated painting platform rather than generic FSM. PaintScout and Estimate Rocket lead on painter-specific estimating (surface-based pricing, proposals); QuoteIQ and CorkCRM are painting CRMs/all-in-ones that add lead pipeline, scheduling, and review collection. Painters who want a single generalist tool also use Jobber or Housecall Pro, but the dedicated tools price jobs the way painters actually do.

vertical_faqs.q: How much does painting software cost?
vertical_faqs.a: It ranges widely. QuoteIQ starts at $29.99/mo and CorkCRM around $50/mo; Estimate Rocket runs roughly $59-$99/mo; PaintScout is $119/user/mo for estimating, or $168/user/mo with the CRM add-on. Estimating-only tools sit at the lower end; full CRM/all-in-one capability costs more, and per-user pricing (PaintScout) scales with crew size.

vertical_faqs.q: Do painters need dedicated software or will general FSM work?
vertical_faqs.a: General FSM (Jobber, Housecall Pro) works for many painters, especially those who want one tool across services. But dedicated painting software prices jobs by surface type and production rates the way painters actually estimate, produces painter-specific proposals, and increasingly adds AI paint calculators and before/after imagery. If estimating speed and accuracy are your bottleneck, a painting-specific tool usually pays off.

verticals.name: Flooring
verticals.industry_context:
Flooring is a materials-heavy, measurement-driven trade, and the software flooring businesses buy reflects that profit is made or lost on two things: an accurate takeoff and tight control of inventory. Estimators price by area and surface, but the real margin risk is material — seams, pattern repeat, dye lots, remnants, and waste factor mean a small measurement error or a mismanaged roll can erase a job's profit. That is why flooring has its own dedicated software stack rather than relying on generic field-service tools: a takeoff-and-estimating specialist (MeasureSquare) to get material quantities right, and full dealer ERPs (RFMS, QFloors, RollMaster) to run point-of-sale, roll-goods inventory, ordering, scheduling, and accounting as one system.

The category splits by role and size. Takeoff/estimating tools optimize the measurement and quote and feed the rest of the stack. Dealer ERPs run the whole store and scale from a single location to large multi-store, multi-company operations. A newer middle tier of approachable all-in-ones (Floorzap) targets small-to-mid dealers who find enterprise ERPs too heavy, and marketing-led platforms (Broadlume) lead with websites and demand generation and extend into operations. Increasingly, room visualization — letting a shopper preview a floor in their own space — is becoming part of the buying decision; QFloors' 2025 acquisition by Roomvo folded visualization directly into the ERP.
verticals.feature_priorities: Flooring dealers weigh software around materials and measurement. Takeoff and estimating accuracy comes first — area and surface-based pricing with seam, pattern-repeat, and waste-factor logic, since the material estimate is where flooring margin is won or lost. Inventory and roll-goods management is a close second and is unusually demanding: tracking rolls, remnants, dye lots, and special orders is the difference between protecting and bleeding margin. Job costing and real-time profitability visibility come next, followed by point-of-sale/quoting and order processing. Scheduling and install-crew coordination, plus customer management, round out daily operations. Accounting (native in the dealer ERPs, or QuickBooks integration for lighter tools) and room visualization — increasingly expected at the showroom and online — complete the picture. Buyers differ mainly on whether they need a takeoff specialist plus accounting, an approachable all-in-one, or a full multi-location dealer ERP, and on appetite for implementation cost and complexity.

vertical_faqs.q: What software do flooring dealers use?
vertical_faqs.a: Flooring businesses typically use a dedicated stack rather than generic field-service tools. MeasureSquare is the standard for takeoff and estimating (precise material and waste calculation), while RFMS, QFloors, and RollMaster are full dealer ERPs that run point-of-sale, roll-goods inventory, ordering, scheduling, and accounting. Smaller dealers may use an approachable all-in-one like Floorzap, and Broadlume leads with websites and marketing. Many dealers pair MeasureSquare for estimating with an ERP or QuickBooks for the back office.

vertical_faqs.q: How much does flooring software cost?
vertical_faqs.a: It ranges widely. Takeoff/estimating like MeasureSquare runs $49-$179/user/month by edition. Approachable all-in-ones like Floorzap are roughly $375-$399/month flat plus setup, and QFloors starts at $55-$89/month plus a $1,500 setup fee. Enterprise dealer ERPs (RFMS, RollMaster) and marketing-led platforms (Broadlume) do not publish pricing — expect custom quotes in the low thousands per month plus implementation fees of several thousand dollars.

vertical_faqs.q: Do I need a full ERP or just estimating software?
vertical_faqs.a: It depends on size and pain point. If your bottleneck is fast, accurate takeoffs, an estimating specialist like MeasureSquare plus QuickBooks may be enough. If you are losing visibility into inventory, orders, and margin across the store — or running multiple locations — a dealer ERP (QFloors for small-to-mid, RFMS or RollMaster for larger) consolidates everything but costs more and takes real implementation. Many dealers run a takeoff tool and an ERP together.

vertical_faqs.q: Does flooring software include room visualization?
vertical_faqs.a: Increasingly, yes. Room visualization — letting a shopper preview a floor in a photo of their own room — has become part of the buying decision. QFloors bundles it directly after its 2025 acquisition by Roomvo, and Broadlume includes customer-facing website and visualization tooling. Standalone visualization platforms also integrate with dealer websites. If showroom and online selling matter to you, weigh visualization alongside back-office features.

verticals.name: Waste & Junk Removal
verticals.industry_context:
Waste and junk businesses run on routes and assets, not just visits, and the software they buy reflects that. Whether the work is septic pumping, grease-trap cleaning, portable-toilet rental, roll-off dumpsters, junk removal, or commercial trash routes, the operation revolves around trucks, drivers, recurring schedules, and physical assets (tanks, toilets, dumpsters, containers) that get placed, serviced, and picked up. That is why the category has dedicated software — ServiceCore for the liquid-waste and container space, Docket and Starlight for dumpster/roll-off and hauling, Trash Flow for established modular hauler billing, and Routeware at the enterprise/municipal end — rather than relying on generic field-service tools. The center of gravity is scheduling and dispatch, route optimization, asset/container tracking, and recurring billing.

The category splits by segment and scale. Dedicated all-in-ones (ServiceCore) and dumpster/hauling specialists (Docket, Starlight, Trash Flow) serve owner-operated and mid-size haulers, while enterprise platforms (Routeware) serve large municipal and private collection operations. At the smaller, residential junk-removal end, many operators run a generalist field-service platform — Workiz, Housecall Pro, or Jobber — because their need is closer to standard scheduling, dispatch, and invoicing than to container-asset tracking. Pricing across the dedicated tools is typically per-truck and quote-based rather than published, so fleet size drives cost, and several vendors require a demo before sharing a number.
verticals.feature_priorities: Waste and junk operators weigh software around routes, assets, and recurring revenue. Scheduling and dispatch come first — assigning jobs to trucks and drivers efficiently and adjusting in real time. Route optimization is close behind, since fuel and drive time are major costs and tighter routes directly improve margin. Asset and container tracking is distinctive to this category: knowing where every dumpster, portable toilet, or tank is placed and when it is due protects revenue and prevents lost or unbilled units. Recurring and automated billing matters because much of the revenue is repeat service, and accurate high-volume invoicing (plus online payments) is a frequent buying driver. A reliable driver mobile app with turn-by-turn directions, customer payment options and portals, and QuickBooks/accounting integration round out the core. Buyers differ mainly on segment (liquid-waste vs dumpster vs municipal collection vs residential junk) and scale, which determines whether a dedicated per-truck platform or a generalist FSM is the right fit.

vertical_faqs.q: What software do junk removal, dumpster, and septic businesses use?
vertical_faqs.a: It depends on the segment. Liquid-waste and container businesses (septic, grease, portable toilets, dumpsters) commonly use ServiceCore; roll-off and hauling operations use Docket, Starlight Software, or Trash Flow; large municipal/private collection uses enterprise platforms like Routeware. Smaller residential junk-removal operators often run a generalist field-service tool such as Workiz, Housecall Pro, or Jobber, since their needs are closer to standard scheduling and invoicing than container-asset tracking.

vertical_faqs.q: How much does waste/junk removal software cost?
vertical_faqs.a: Most dedicated waste platforms (ServiceCore, Docket, Starlight, Trash Flow) do not publish pricing and use per-truck or modular quote-based models, so cost scales with fleet size and usually requires a demo. Enterprise waste software like Routeware can run $100K+/year. At the smaller end, generalist FSM tools used for junk removal (Workiz, Housecall Pro, Jobber) have published plans typically in the tens-to-low-hundreds of dollars per month.

vertical_faqs.q: Do I need dedicated waste software or a generalist field-service tool?
vertical_faqs.a: It comes down to whether you manage physical assets and routes or mostly jobs. If you place and track containers (dumpsters, portable toilets) or run recurring routes with per-truck economics, a dedicated platform (ServiceCore, Docket, Starlight, Trash Flow) is built for that. If you run a residential junk-removal crew whose needs are scheduling, dispatch, and invoicing, a generalist FSM (Workiz, Housecall Pro, Jobber) is often simpler and cheaper.

vertical_faqs.q: Why does asset and container tracking matter so much?
vertical_faqs.a: For dumpster, portable-toilet, and septic businesses, the containers and tanks themselves are the revenue-generating assets. Knowing exactly where each unit is placed, how long it has been out, and when it is due for service prevents lost or unbilled units and keeps recurring revenue accurate. Generic field-service tools that treat each job as a one-off visit miss this, which is why dedicated waste platforms emphasize asset tracking.
